title: Secrets do not belong in build logs
hook: A secret leak is not always a dramatic breach. Sometimes it is one helpful debug line in CI, copied into a ticket, indexed forever.

beats.heading: Logs are where secrets go to become public-ish

blocks.html: Build logs travel. They show up in CI, chat, screenshots, support tickets, artifacts, and observability tools. If a token lands there once, your cleanup job is now bigger than the original bug.

blocks.title: ci · bad debug
blocks.caption: The leak is often accidental and very readable.

lines.t: # masked here maybe, copied elsewhere maybe not

beats.heading: Config belongs in the environment, not the repo

blocks.html: The old rule still holds up: keep config that varies by deploy in the environment. Source code should name the secret it needs, not carry the secret itself. That makes rotation, review, and access control possible.

blocks.caption: Fail loudly on missing config. Never print the value.

beats.heading: Masking helps, but rotation is the fix

blocks.html: CI masking is a guardrail, not a guarantee. Tools can detect common tokens, but they cannot understand every homegrown credential or every place a value gets transformed. If a secret may have leaked, revoke it, rotate it, and audit use.

items.label: steps after exposure: revoke, rotate, audit
source of truth for secret ownership

blocks.html: The durable fix is not yelling "be careful" in every PR. It is secret scanning, locked-down CI variables, short-lived credentials, clear runbooks, and tests that fail when code tries to log sensitive values.
